coredata.dc:title: Application of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots composite with quinoline as a solar cells material
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Zainul, Rahadian
Basem, Ali
Altayeh, Aiham O.
Kumar, Nitin
Sharma, Rohit
Al-Bahrani, Mohammed
Alqarni, Sondos Abdullah
Islam, Saiful
Yadav, Anupam
coredata.dc:description: In order to adjust electrochemical and optical attributes that impact performance of dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs), photosensitizer has a critical role. Hence, it must meet crucial needs for effective operation of dye-sensitized solar cells. Present work suggests quinoline (QNL) as a photosensitizer by modifying its attributes via hybridization accompanied by N-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs). In order to study electronic, optical, geometric attributes, density functional theory (DFT) and its time-dependent approach have been employed. In order to prove validity of chosen basis set and functional, available experimental data of parent QNL has been applied. Energy gap (Eg) of QNL has been considerably narrowed via hybridization. Hence, its absorption has been altered from ultraviolet range to visible range that corresponds to solar spectra. Furthermore, an increment of absorption intensity resulted in high light-harvesting efficiency (LHE) that can enhance generation of electrical current. Redox potential and conduction band have been suitably matched with energy levels of created dye nanocomposites, demonstrating possibility of electron regeneration and injection. Noted traits validate that described substances display appealing features, making them potentially suitable for use in DSSCs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Quinoline
Dye-sensitized solar cells
Graphene quantum dots
Light-harvesting efficiency

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Optimized geometries of isolated QNL, GQD, and both nanocomposites with their molecular electrostatic maps (MEPs).
Binding energies of all designed nanocomposites.
Energy levels alignment diagram for isolated catechin, all the nanocomposites, TiO2 CB, and I−/I−3 redox potential.
DOS graph of N1-G-FQNL (a) and N2-G-QNL (b).
The vertical excited energies and their oscillator strengths calculated at M06-2X/6–31 + G (d,p) with CPCM, methanol solvent.
Emission transitions wavelengths λ max in nm, oscillator strength f, Stokes shift λ ss in nm, and short-circuit current density JSC in mA.cm −2.

head.ce:title: Application of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots composite with quinoline as a solar cells material

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In order to adjust electrochemical and optical attributes that impact performance of dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs), photosensitizer has a critical role. Hence, it must meet crucial needs for effective operation of dye-sensitized solar cells. Present work suggests quinoline (QNL) as a photosensitizer by modifying its attributes via hybridization accompanied by N-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs). In order to study electronic, optical, geometric attributes, density functional theory (DFT) and its time-dependent approach have been employed. In order to prove validity of chosen basis set and functional, available experimental data of parent QNL has been applied. Energy gap (Eg) of QNL has been considerably narrowed via hybridization. Hence, its absorption has been altered from ultraviolet range to visible range that corresponds to solar spectra. Furthermore, an increment of absorption intensity resulted in high light-harvesting efficiency (LHE) that can enhance generation of electrical current. Redox potential and conduction band have been suitably matched with energy levels of created dye nanocomposites, demonstrating possibility of electron regeneration and injection. Noted traits validate that described substances display appealing features, making them potentially suitable for use in DSSCs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Using energy on a daily basis poses a threat to available energy resources, making it crucial to explore alternative sources of renewable energy. A promising option is solar energy, where photons are adsorbed from solar radiation and transformed into electric energy by photovoltaic solar cells (SCs) [1–6]. Third-generation SCs have been classified into four types based on technology employed, namely concentrated SCs, dye-sensitized, polymer-based, and nanocrystal-based [7–10]. Although these innovative technologies have been presented and utilized to enhance efficiency of SCs, their commercialization is hindered by their instability during long-term operation and short lifespan [11]. Because of environmentally friendly properties, simple materials synthesis, low price, dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) are regarded one of the most encouraging SCs [12–15]. These solar cells consist of a redox electrolyte, a counter electrode, and a semiconductor photoanode as a working electrode that is covered by a layer of dye/photosensitizer [1,2,16–18].

In recent years, researchers have become interested in using natural dyes on a TiO2 photoanode in DSSCs [4,19]. One type of DSSC was fabricated by Singh's research group employing three anthocyanin dyes extracted from pomegranate (POM) fruit, Melastoma malabathricum (MM) fruit, Begonia malabarica Lam. (BM) leaves in C2H5OH without purification [3]. Results showed that open-circuit voltage (OCV) and short-circuit current density (JSC) values increased in the order of MM < POM < BM, leading to an increase in power conversion efficiency in similar order [3]. Without additional modification, Güzel et al. also used natural dye extracts from Phytolacca americana stalk (SPA), Phytolacca americana fruit (FPA), Berberis vulgaris root (RBV), Berberis vulgaris fruit (FBV) [19]. For RBV, FBV, as well as both FPA and SPA, main dyes in each extract are isoquinoline alkaloid, anthocyanin, and betalain, respectively. Electrochemical and optical attributes were observed in the extracts that make them appropriate to employ as sensitizers in dye-sensitized solar cells. SPA and FPA showed wider absorption peaks, resulting in higher JSC values and better light-harvesting capacity compared to RBV and FBV. These observations led to superior performance of DSSC. Open-circuit voltage values followed a resembling trend, and the highest efficiency of 3.04 % was achieved using SPA due to improvements in both OCV and JSC [19].

Although natural dyes have fascinating attributes and benefits, they experience a low efficiency in converting power [7]. Lately, investigations have shown that introducing graphene quantum dots (GQDs) can improve LHE of photosensitizers [20–22]. Nanocomposites of various natural dyes hybridized with GQD have been developed by Gao and colleagues [20–22]. Six different dyes consisting of carbazole-carbazole and various π-linkers were combined with GQD (graphene quantum dots) and studied in a THF (tetrahydrofuran) solution using a polarizable continuum model (CPM) at two different levels of computational theory: TD-CAM-B3LYP/6-311+G(d,p) and B3LYP/6-31G (d,p) [21]. When natural dyes were combined with GQDs, the resulting nanocomposites had larger driving forces (−2.27 eV to −2.32 eV) compared to isolated dyes (−1.43 eV to −2.06 eV). This led to a stronger absorption peak and a higher LHE (0.995%–0.998 %) in the nanocomposites compared to isolated carbazole-carbazole dyes (0.916%–0.971 %). As a result, the nanocomposites with higher LHE were predicted to have a greater JSC compared to isolated dyes [22]. It has been reported that the TPP (tetraphenyl-porphyrin) dye is suitable for use as a sensitizer in DSSCs [22]. Theoretical analysis was carried out on TPP nanocomposites in a THF solvent after being combined with GQD and GOQD, which had different numbers of hydroxyl groups. All of the resulting nanocomposites showed excellent optical properties and charge separation capabilities. However, GOQD-(18OH)-TPP showed the most potential as a sensitizer due to its impressive properties, which included the highest LHE (0.998 %), an OCV of 1.47 eV, and a high driving force (−2.44 eV) [22]. One type of dye-sensitized solar cell has been produced by combining phenoxazine-based dyes with pyrrole (POXP) and benzene (POXB) π-spacers with graphene quantum dot [20]. Another research work involved combining GQD with eight anthoxanthin dyes (Quercetin, Kaempferol, Galangin, Chrysin, 3-Hydroxyflavone, Luteolin, and Apigenin) in tetrahydrofuran with CPM [2]. Absorption maxima of isolated dyes (ranging from 187.19 to 363.81 nm) have been shifted to longer wavelengths (ranging from 449.05 to 456.42 nm) with increased intensities up to 1.7432 by obtained nanocomposites. Therefore, light-harvesting efficiency was enhanced from 0.6396%-0.6396 % to 0.9593%–0.9819 %. The two most promising nanocomposites are GQD-Chrysin and GQD-Flavone, as they are only ones that exhibit proper alignment with conduction bond of TiO2 subsequent to being coupled with it [2]. Heterocyclic compounds (HCs) are cyclic compounds that contain a minimum of two types of atoms in their ring structure, usually carbon and non-metals such as O, S, and N. Depending on the electronic attributes of rings, HCs can be categorized as either aromatic or non-aromatic [23]. Aromatic hydrocarbons are required to follow Huckel's rule, which states that any aromatic compound must contain a ring system with a total of 4n + 2 pi electrons. Examples of such compounds include quinoline, thiophene, pyridine, and furan. On the other hand, non-aromatic heterocycles do not possess aromatic ring structures, such as thietane, thiirane, and tetrahydrofuran. Quinoline is a nitrogen-containing aromatic hydrocarbon [24–26]. It can be dissolved in organic solvents like ethanol, methanol, and water because it is an organic molecule. The compound has a maximum absorbance in the 220–300 nm range [27].

The focus of this work is on creating nanocomposite materials by combining QNL with GQD. Additionally, present research aimed to functionalize GQD by doping it with N atoms. Twelve different nanocomposites were designed and compared to QNL and each other in terms of various attributes, including intramolecular charge transfer (ICT), reorganization energies, binding energies, electrochemical parameters, emission attributes, density of states (DOS), and frontier molecular orbitals (FMOs). The hybridization of QNL with GQD resulted in a shift in Eg, allowing for improved absorption of isolated catechin in visible area. As a result, all QLN-GQD nanocomposites were found to be effective photosensitizers in DSSCs.

DFT computations have been applied for optimization of these molecules at M06-2X/6-311G(d,p) [28,29] level employing GAMESS software [30]. A search was conducted to determine the most stable structure due to the fact that these molecules exhibit various conformations. To confirm that optimized structure is consistent with minimum energy point on potential energy surface (PES), frequency computations were carried out subsequent to optimization. In order to select a reliable theoretical approach, the highest absorption value of a reference molecule has been adjusted to match experimentally determined highest absorption value. M06-2X functional was found to produce absorption values that closely match experimental results, and therefore it has been utilized in junction with 6-311+G(d,p) for time-dependent DFT computations in this study. To consider the influence of solvation on the absorption spectra in methanol solvent, self-consistent reaction field-polarizable continuum model (SCRF-PCM) was applied [31]. QNL molecule exhibited a maximum absorption wavelength of 225 nm in experimental data [32]. Computed values were all slightly lower than experimental value, with only minor differences. GaussSum software was utilized to compute and generate DOS diagrams [33]. DOS diagrams visually represent Eg values and energy levels, aiding in comprehension of how hybridization influences them. Adjusting Eg values is responsible for altering various photo and electro parameters, including absorption maximum. Stability of nanocomposite is indicated by binding energy (Eb), which enhances stability by its increment. Binding energy is computed as followings [34]:
(1)
E b
=
[
(
n
E C
+ n
E O
+
E N
)
−
E
s y s t e m
]
t o t a l
n u m b e r
o f
a t o m s
Herein, EN, EO, EH, EC, and Esystem are atomic energies of nitrogen, oxygen, hydrogen, carbon, and energy of system, respectively.

Capability to capture sunlight, which enhances JSC, is quantified by LHE. LHE is determined as follows, in which maximum oscillator strength corresponding to the highest wavelength of absorption peak is represented by f [34,35]:
(2)
L H E = 1 −
10
− f

JSC may be calculated using Eq. (3) [36] where ηcoll is charge collection efficiency and φinj is efficiency of electron injection; φph.AM1.5G denotes standard reference of solar radiation intensity in mAcm−2nm−1.
(3)
J
s c
= e ∫ L H E
( λ )
φ
i n j
η
c o l l
φ
p h . A M I 1.5 G
( λ )
d
( λ )
OCV refers to maximum voltage attained when there isn't current flowing through solar cell. In theory, it relies on electron injection from photosensitizer donor's lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) to acceptor semiconductor TiO2 electrode's conduction band (CB). OCV is computed using Eq. (4), in which e, ELUMO, and ECB signify electron charge (1 eV), LUMO energy of photosensitizer, and CB energy of TiO2, respectively [36].
(4)
V
O C
=
E
L U M O
−
E
C B
e
Power conversion efficiency (η) of a solar cell is closely linked to its fill factor (FF), JSC, and OCV, all of which contribute to the maximum output power (Pmax). Equation (5) is used to calculate η, where Pin refers to intensity of incident solar radiation [37].
(5)
η =
F F
V
o c
J
S c
P
i n c
=
P max
P
i n c

Fig. 1 displays optimized structures of isolated QNL and isolated GQD nanocomposites, along with molecular electrostatic potential (MEP) maps of whole nanocomposites. The first modeled nanocomposite in present research is GQD hybridized with catechin (G-QNL). As depicted in Fig. 2 , composite introduces some polarity to nanocomposite lattice, thereby enhancing its electron transfer attributes. Nitrogen doping was performed on G-QNL nanocomposites using two configurations: central substitutional nitrogen (N1-G-QNL) and terminal nitrogen doping at the edge (N2-G-QNL). Introduction of N into GQD lattice results in p-type attributes that enhance hole mobility of nanocomposites.

Eb values of designed nanocomposites are displayed in Fig. 2. All systems are stable, as revealed by their positive Eb values ranging from 7.28 to 7.79 eV, with the influence of nitrogen doping being insignificant. In-plane doped composite systems possess greater Eb values due to undistorted sp2 hybridization of GQD lattice. Agnoli and Favaro reported that N doping at edge is more stable than at center, with less induced strain [38]. Hence, N2-G-QNL composite with edge doping exhibits greater values of Eb (7.79 eV) than N1-G-QNL composite with central doping (7.21 eV).

Fig. 3 displays FMOs of various photosensitizers and their agreement with CB of TiO2 and I‾/I‾₃ RP. In a DSSC, there are two electron transfer processes: dye regeneration and electron injection. According to Fig. 3, both processes are spontaneous. Specifically, LUMOs of photosensitizers inject excited free electrons into CB, while electrons are simultaneously regenerated from RP to HOMOs of photosensitizers to reduce oxidized dye. For electrons to be transferred, HOMOs and LUMOs of nanocomposites must be more negative than RP and less negative than CB, respectively. Fig. 3 demonstrates that LUMOs of all nanocomposites (−3.72 eV to −2.38 eV) are less negative than conduction band (4.3 eV) [35], thus confirming electron injection process. All nanocomposites have HOMOs between −5.93 and −4.91 eV, which are more negative than RP (−4.8 eV) [34]. This indicates that all systems are capable of electron/dye regeneration. Fig. 3 reveals that LUMO of QNL was stabilized by its hybridization with GQD, and that further stabilization of G-QNL has been achieved through N doping. Stability of LUMOs of all nanocomposites was also enhanced by N doping. With reduction of distance (energy difference) between CB and LUMOs of nanocomposites, it becomes more simple to inject electrons into CB because less energy is required to transfer an electron [39]. Stabilizing energy levels that match HOMO and LUMO of photosensitizer with electrolyte and CB and the, respectively, can prevent energy loss [40]. Likewise, as separation between RP and HOMOs of nanocomposites reduces, regeneration of electrons becomes simpler. LUMO of QNL is 3.53 eV away from CB, while energy difference between CB (EL-CB) of all nanocomposites and LUMOs of photosensitizers is less than or equal to 1.50 eV. This significant stabilization of energy levels prevents recombination of electrons and improves injection of them. Energy difference between HOMOs of QNL (ERP-H) and RP is 1.99 eV, whereas HOMOs of all nanocomposites are at most 0.42 eV below RP. This implies that HOMOs of the nanocomposites are better suited as photosensitizers in comparison to isolated QNL to regenerate electrons from RP. According to Fig. 3, difference between LUMO of N2-G-QNL and CB is only 1.34 eV, indicating that their alignment is the most suitable. In contrast, N1-G-QNL is 1.82 eV away from CB. A lower bandgap (Eg) requires less energy to move electrons from their ground state to an excited state, making it easier to capture light and resulting in higher efficiency. QNL (quantum nitride lattice) has a large bandgap of over 5 eV, which gives it an insulating property, as depicted in Fig. 3. However, combining QNL with GQD reduced its Eg by more than 50 %, inducing a semi conductivity that increased light-capturing and electron excitation. N-doping further decreased Eg of G-QNL from 2.98 eV to 2.56–1.95 eV N1-G-QNL showed the most significant reduction in Eg of QNL by 34.5 %. Generally, doping resulted in lower energy gap values, demonstrating capability to efficiently tune Eg of nanocomposites. Adsorption wavelengths and Eg values are inversely related, so altering Eg can affect adsorption wavelengths, resulting in either shorter or longer wavelengths. As a result, it is anticipated that all nanocomposites will cause a shift towards longer wavelengths, specifically in infrared region, in terms of their absorption.

N is an atom with three valence electrons, which can bond with three C atoms and leave a vacancy (hole) on the fourth site of it. When N is doped into the GQD lattice, it creates holes that act as charge carriers, recognized as p-type doping. This type of doping results in creation of new unoccupied energy states top of HOMOs, which reduces Eg Ref. [41]. Newly created energy states receive electrons from HOMO, which creates holes. In addition, number of holes and energy states are equal. In Fig. 4 , DOS for edge and central doping with B is indicated. As revealed, a new unoccupied energy state is created by N top of HOMO level. A novel energy level resulting from edge doping (N2-G-QNL) overlaps with HOMO level, while the one resulting from central doping (N1-G-QNL) is higher than HOMO level. This leads to a wider Eg of central doping (1.95 eV) compared to edge doping (2.56 eV). Therefore, center doping may result in a more considerable bathochromic shift in UV–vis absorption.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Emission transition analysis and UV–vis absorption
ce:section.ce:para: The efficiency of DSSCs is closely linked to the generation of current, which is affected by light absorption. Therefore, the optical properties of DSSCs play a crucial role in improving their effectiveness. Photosensitizers used in DSSCs should have intense absorption in visible range between 400 and 800 nm, this refers to the ability of a material or a substance to absorb photons within the range of solar radiation, which causes the excitation of its electrons. In order to start current generation, electron excitation is necessary, which is then injected into CB of photoanode and flows as a current. For all systems applying CPCM-M06-2X/6-31+G(d,p) in methanol solvent, the highest absorption wavelengths are listed in Table 1 . QNL delivers the highest absorption at 205 nm in UV area (100–300 nm), which is in close agreement with experimental observations (225 nm) and has a difference of only 20 nm. Combining QNL with GQD causes a redshift in absorption towards longer wavelengths and a shift from the UV to visible range at 446 nm. This indicates that G-QNL can act as a photosensitizer and absorb photons within the solar radiation range, exciting its electrons in the process. According to Table 1, redshift is caused by Eg decrease of QNL. Doping a central N atom in G-QNL (N1-G-QNL) causes a redshift of absorption from 446 nm to 479 nm, while edge N-doping (N2-G-QNL) results in a negligible redshift of absorption to 453 nm. As anticipated from FMOs and DOS plots, N1-G-QNL displays longer wavelengths compared to N2-G-QNL. Nevertheless, both nanocomposites have absorption in visible area, distinguishing them from QNL and confirming their potential as photosensitizers.

Occurrence of a transition is attributed to transition dipole moment, square of which is directly proportional to oscillator strength [42]. A higher oscillator strength intensity increases possibility of transition occurring and is also connected to light-harvesting efficiency. An increase in LHE results in better photon capture ability, leading to generation of more current. As indicated in Table 1, G-QNL exhibits transitions at 446 nm with oscillator strengths of 1.23, which have more intensity compared to QNL transitions. This increment raises LHE from 0.56 to 0.95, making G-QNL a superior photon capturer. In contrast, absorption intensity of N1-G-QNL and N2-G-QNL drops to 0.87 and 0.82, respectively, resulting in a decline in LHE. G-QNL has the highest LHE and absorption intensity. Nevertheless, LHE of both nanocomposites is close to unity, indicating that they are both capable of capturing photons effectively. Short circuit current density, excited state lifetime, oscillator strength, excitation energy, and maximum emission wavelengths are listed in Table 2 . For both nanocomposites, maximum emission wavelengths were observed to be at longer wavelengths compared to their absorption maxima. Difference between emission and absorption peaks is known as Stokes shift, which is indicative of difference between the ground and excited state geometries as well as strength of ICT character [43]. Stokes shift of G-QNL ranges from 70 nm to 81 nm for N1-GQNL. Furthermore, doping results in an increase in Stokes shift of both nanocomposites, indicating that it assists in shifting emission peaks towards longer wavelengths.

Based on Eq. (5), there is a direct relationship between power conversion efficiency (η) and JSC of DSSCs. Therefore, power conversion efficiency can be improved by increasing generated current. As listed in Table 2, both nanocomposites produce similar JSC values ranging from 8.82 to 17.68 mA cm−2. N1-G-QNL system has the highest JSC value of 16.56 mAcm−2, which may be owing to its higher photon flux. In contrast, edge and central doping with B in N2-G-QNL results in a lower ability to generate current, with only 12.87 mA cm−2. Therefore, doping B in central region of N1-G-QNL may result in a higher η compared to edge doping in N2-G-QNL. Additionally, since N1-G-QNL generates the highest amount of current, it is expected to possess the highest η among the two nanocomposites and is deemed the most favorable option.

In present DFT/TD-DFT investigation, various parameters have been computed to assess suitability of QNL and its nanocomposites as photosensitizers in DSSCs. Impact of hybridization of GQD with QNL and N doping has been investigated. All designed nanocomposites were deemed stable based on their positive Eb values. The HOMOs and LUMOs of nanocomposites were properly aligned with RP and CB, respectively. N1-G-QNL and N2-G-QNL were identified as nanocomposites with the highest potential for electron transfer due to their LUMO and HOMO energy levels being the closest to the CB and RP, respectively. Visible region wavelengths of QNL were enhanced by significantly decreasing Eg, which shifted absorption of QNL from UV region to visible region in range of 479 nm. Consequently, most of the doped nanocomposites had higher LHE than the undoped systems, potentially leading to higher current production. Moreover, doping demonstrated better current generation than undoped systems, with N1-G-QNL producing the highest JSC of 17.68 mA cm−2. Increased current production would improve η value of a DSSC.

Rahadian Zainul: Data curation, Formal analysis, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Ali Basem: Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Aiham O. Altayeh: Investigation, Methodology, Software, Writing – original draft. Nitin Kumar: Data curation, Formal analysis, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Rohit Sharma: Software, Validation, Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Mohammed Al-Bahrani: Investigation, Methodology, Resources, Writing – original draft. Sondos Abdullah Alqarni: Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Software, Writing – original draft. Saiful Islam: Data curation, Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Writing – original draft. Anupam Yadav: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors extend their appreciation to the Deanship of Research and Graduate Studies at King Khalid University for funding this work through large group Research Project under grant number RGP2/260/45

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Efficiency enhancement of dye-sensitized solar cells based on Gracilaria/Ulva using graphene quantum dot
